classes.name: SABA (Rescue)
classes.description: Short-acting beta-agonist for acute bronchospasm; quick relief, not a controller.
classes.plainDescription: The inhaler you grab when you suddenly can't breathe. Works in minutes.

classes.name: ICS (Maintenance)
classes.description: Inhaled corticosteroid; the anti-inflammatory foundation of daily asthma control.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that calms airway inflammation so attacks happen less.

classes.name: ICS/LABA (Combo)
classes.description: Steroid + long-acting bronchodilator in one inhaler for moderate-to-severe asthma or COPD.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that combines the steroid with a long-acting opener.

classes.name: LAMA
classes.description: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist. FDA-indicated for COPD maintenance, not asthma monotherapy; not for rescue.
classes.plainDescription: A once-daily opener used mainly for COPD — not a rescue inhaler.

classes.name: Biologics & Non-Inhalers
classes.description: Specialty-pharmacy biologics (anti-IgE, anti-IL5, anti-IL4) for severe asthma. Separate PA pathway; not inhalers.
classes.plainDescription: Injection or infusion drugs for severe asthma — handled differently from inhalers.

glossary.short: Insurer sign-off needed before the drug is covered.
glossary.definition: A requirement that the prescriber get the plan's approval before it will pay for a drug. Until it's approved, the pharmacy claim rejects — which is the round-trip this tool helps you skip.

glossary.short: The plan forces the brand; the generic rejects.
glossary.definition: Some plans prefer a specific brand and won't cover its generic equivalent. You must write the brand and mark "dispense as written" or the pharmacy claim rejects. Example: MassHealth prefers brand Ventolin over generic albuterol.

glossary.short: Must fail a preferred drug first.
glossary.definition: The plan requires the patient to try (and fail, or be intolerant of) one or more preferred drugs before it will cover the one you want.

glossary.short: Tell the pharmacy not to substitute a generic.
glossary.definition: A note on the prescription instructing the pharmacist to dispense the exact brand you wrote, not a generic substitute. Needed when the plan's BOGL rule forces the brand.
Short-acting beta-agonist. The inhaler a patient carries and uses when they suddenly can't breathe well. Works in minutes; not a daily controller.

glossary.short: A daily steroid inhaler that calms inflammation.
glossary.definition: Inhaled corticosteroid. Taken every day to keep the airways from getting inflamed and twitchy. It prevents attacks; it does not relieve one in progress.

glossary.short: Inhalers have generics; biologics have biosimilars.
glossary.definition: Inhalers are small-molecule drugs, so cheaper copies are FDA-approved "generics" (e.g., Wixela Inhub for Advair). Injectable biologics like Xolair get "biosimilars" instead (e.g., Omlyclo) — a different FDA pathway.

classes.name: Estrogen — transdermal
classes.description: Transdermal 17β-estradiol (patch, gel, or spray); skips first-pass hepatic metabolism, lower VTE risk than oral.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen patch or gel absorbed through the skin — gentler on clot risk than pills.

classes.name: Estrogen — oral
classes.description: Oral estradiol or conjugated estrogens; effective, but first-pass hepatic metabolism raises VTE/clot and stroke risk vs transdermal.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen pill taken by mouth. Works well, but carries a higher clot risk than the patch.

classes.name: Progestogen
classes.description: Progestogen added to systemic estrogen for endometrial protection in patients with an intact uterus; micronized progesterone preferred.
classes.plainDescription: Added to estrogen if you still have a uterus, to protect the uterine lining.

classes.name: Vaginal (local) estrogen
classes.description: Low-dose vaginal estradiol (cream, tablet, insert, or ring) for genitourinary syndrome of menopause; minimal systemic absorption.
classes.plainDescription: Low-dose estrogen placed vaginally for dryness and urinary symptoms (GSM).

classes.name: Estrogen–progestin (oral combo)
classes.description: A single oral tablet with both an estrogen and a progestin, for a person with an intact uterus; generic estradiol/norethindrone acetate is the usual first-pass.
classes.plainDescription: One pill that combines estrogen and the uterus-protecting hormone.

Pages 19-22 (Cardiovascular Agents section) list every ACE inhibitor generic name and brand alphabetically with Tier and Requirements/Limits columns: benazepril hcl oral (Tier 1), captopril oral (Tier 1), enalapril maleate oral solution (Tier 3, PA), enalapril maleate oral tablet (Tier 1), fosinopril sodium (Tier 1), lisinopril oral (Tier 1), ramipril (Tier 1), trandolapril (Tier 1) - all with no PA/ST/QL codes except the enalapril oral solution. Brand entries ALTACE, VASOTEC, VASERETIC, ZESTRIL, ZESTORETIC are marked 'E' and LOTENSIN/LOTENSIN HCT are Tier 4; the legend on page 5 defines 'E' as 'May be excluded from coverage or subject to Prior Authorization in Connecticut, New Jersey and New York' - directly applicable since this is the NY-scoped cell and the document's footer on every drug-list page states 'CT, NJ and NY.' No quinapril/Accupril, moexipril/Univasc, or perindopril/Aceon entries exist anywhere in the 80-page document (confirmed via full-text scan) - they are simply absent from this PDL, not merely unlisted due to a search gap. Preferred agent selection (lisinopril) is inferred rather than explicitly named 'preferred' in prose - the PDL lists all Tier 1 generics as equally unrestricted with no single agent singled out as 'preferred' by name; lisinopril is the most commonly prescribed ACE inhibitor in practice and carries no distinguishing restriction here, consistent with every other Tier-1 generic in the class. Representative strength (10 mg) and sig are standard prescribing convention, not read from the PDL (which does not include dosing directions). BOGL (brand-over-generic) is not active for this class: all Tier 1 generics are unrestricted while every brand name in the class is either excluded/PA-flagged (E) or pushed to Tier 4, i.e. the formulary favors generic, not brand.

The full class list is: benazepril hcl (Tier 1, QL), captopril (Tier 2, QL), enalapril maleate (Tier 1, QL), fosinopril sodium (Tier 1, QL), lisinopril (Tier 1, QL), moexipril hcl (Tier 1, QL), perindopril erbumine (Tier 1, QL), quinapril hcl (Tier 1, QL), ramipril 1.25mg (Tier 1, DO=dose optimization), ramipril 10mg/2.5mg/5mg (Tier 1, QL), and trandolapril (Tier 1, QL) — no brand names appear anywhere in this section (confirmed: the document states on page 3 this is 'a complete listing of all the drugs on the drug list'). None of the 10 agents carry a PA, ST (step therapy), or NF (non-formulary) flag per the legend on page 27 ('PA=Prior Authorization QL=Quantity Limit ST=Step Therapy DO=Dose Optimization'), so paRequired is empty and stepTherapy is null for this class — confirmed, not inferred. Lisinopril was selected as the representative preferred agent because it is the single most commonly prescribed first-line ACE inhibitor and sits at the lowest tier (Tier 1) alongside all agents except captopril; the document itself does not single out one ACE inhibitor as 'preferred' beyond the shared Tier 1 designation, so the choice of lisinopril as the representative pick (vs. e.g. enalapril) is inferred, not stated verbatim — hence noting this distinction though the overall record is fully verified against the source. records.verificationNote: Read directly off the 'Excellus BlueCross BlueShield 2026 Formulary (List of Covered Drugs)' PDF covering Medicare BlueActive (PPO) and its sibling Medicare Advantage plans (fetched via direct HTTPS request to fm.formularynavigator.com; 108-page, 3.2 MB PDF with real extractable text, confirmed a genuine formulary document, not an error page). Page 1 cover states 'This formulary was updated on 7/01/2026', 'Formulary ID 26059', document code 'Y0028_9055_a_C ... B-8970Y26-05', publisher Excellus BlueCross BlueShield, and explicitly lists 'Medicare BlueActive (PPO)' as the first plan on the cover, confirming this is the correct payer/product document. The 'Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme (Ace) Inhibitors' subsection, filed under the 'CARDIOVASCULAR AGENTS' category, spans the bottom of page 50 into page 51 and reads verbatim: benazepril (Tier 1), captopril (Tier 2), enalapril maleate oral tablet (Tier 1), fosinopril (Tier 1), lisinopril (Tier 1), moexipril (Tier 2), perindopril erbumine (Tier 2), quinapril (Tier 1), ramipril (Tier 1), trandolapril (Tier 2) -- every single entry has a blank REQUIREMENTS/LIMITS column (no PA, ST, or QL code), confirmed by cross-checking the same table rows against neighboring drug classes on the same pages that DO show 'PA', 'ST', or 'QL (n per 30 days)' annotations (e.g. EDARBI 'ST', FILSPARI 'PA', CABLIVI 'PA; QL'), so the absence of any code for the ACE inhibitors is a genuine clean-covered status, not a missing-data artifact. The tier legend on page VI (document page 8) defines Tier 1 as 'Preferred Generic: Select generic drugs ... offer clinical and cost savings advantages' and Tier 2 as 'Generic: Most other generic drugs.' No brand-name ACE inhibitor (searched Accupril, Lotensin, Altace, Vasotec) appears anywhere in the 108-page document, confirming no brand-over-generic policy is active for this class -- brands are simply absent from the formulary, consistent with Medicare Part D plans not stocking branded ACE inhibitors once fully genericized. Lisinopril is selected as the representative preferred pick (per this guide's existing NY ACE inhibitor convention, matching the ny-medicaid record) since five of the nine listed agents (benazepril, enalapril, fosinopril, lisinopril, quinapril, ramipril) share identical Tier 1/no-restriction status; lisinopril is the most commonly prescribed first-line agent among that Tier 1 set. No class-wide step-therapy criterion, quantity limit, or PA policy was found for ACE inhibitors in this document. Not verified: whether Excellus/Express Scripts applies any pharmacy-claims-level edit not disclosed in this member-facing formulary (internal PBM edits can occasionally be stricter than the published document), and whether cost-sharing differs across the plan's several PPO/HMO product variants sharing this one formulary (the tier assignment is identical across all listed plans in this document).
